what_it_does: This file is a structured design-substrate analysis of slides 35-49 of the ConceptV pitch-deck corpus. It records a per-slide narrative arc, attention-flow descriptions, a catalog of visual findings with recipes and generator rules, reusable component definitions, and content-sample budgets for the closing / appendix portion of both a 15-page and 30-page deck variant.
observations: Documents 15 slides (p-35 through p-49) spanning the long-deck close, a condensed 15-page deck, and appendix slides.
Provides an 'attention_flow' entry for every slide describing eye-path, focal points, and terminal elements.

Lists 40+ 'findings' grouped by category: gradient-shading-opacity, geometry, type-hierarchy, padding-alignment, color-palette, atom, line-connector, texture, chrome-mode, zone, sequence, attention-verbs.
Each finding includes a 'recipe', 'evidence', 'emergent' boolean, and a prescriptive 'generator_rule'.
Identifies a warm gold/amber (#C9A84C-ish) as the sole accent color, with dark-grey body text and near-black titles.
Defines reusable components such as ProductHeaderRow, TeamMemberCard, EqualPillarRow, StatValueCard, TimelineBar, HatchedSummaryBand, FullBleedContactSlide, SystemHubDiagram.
Notes deliberate slide reuse between long and short decks (p-35 = p-48, p-36 ≈ p-49, p-37 ≈ p-43).
Symbols list is explicitly empty (no code symbols).
standouts: Compression-invariance rule: the same full-detail slides are reused across deck lengths; density is preserved by omitting slides, not simplifying them.
Appendix-as-depth-layer grammar: the short deck ends with a transitional contact slide pointing to appendix slides that reprise long-deck detail.
Hatched summary band appears on every informational slide as a 'slide contract' closing statement.
Central circular medallion is the only circle in a rectangle-dominated layout, giving it hub authority.
Full-bleed architectural photography frames every contact/closing slide, breaking the content grid.
conventions: Follows a fixed JSON schema with categories, names, recipes, evidence, emergent flags, and generator rules.
Uses neutral, descriptive language without ranking or winner declarations.
Maintains file-local scope and avoids cross-file comparisons.
Employs prescriptive 'always/never' generator-rule phrasing for design patterns.
concerns: Content_samples section is truncated, so completeness is unknown.
Generator rules are strong absolutes ('never', 'always') inferred from a small sample of one pitch-deck corpus.
Attention-flow claims are presented as fact without stated validation method.
Pixel-equivalent measurements are approximate and not tied to a known source resolution.
No code symbols are present, so this file is purely design documentation rather than implementation.
notes: A dense, rule-heavy design-system snapshot of the ConceptV pitch deck's closing grammar, treating slides as reusable atoms and encoding visual behavior into generator recipes.
questions: What is the exact slide dimension and source resolution used for pixel-equivalent measurements?
How were the attention-flow sequences validated—eyetracking, heuristic review, or authorial intent?
What criteria distinguish 'emergent' from non-emergent patterns?
How do these rules relate to the earlier parts of the corpus (parts 1 and 2)?
Who is the intended consumer: a layout generator, a human designer, or a reviewer?
Are the actual image assets available for verification?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose is to document the final slides of the pitch-deck corpus as design substrates. The file does exactly that, but goes further by encoding prescriptive generator rules and reusable components. It matches its stated purpose.
apparent_themes: Attention engineering through shape, color, and sequence
intention_signals: The file appears to be trying to reverse-engineer a pitch deck into a reproducible visual language and component library, so that consistent ConceptV-style decks can be generated or audited automatically across long and short formats.
novelty: The explicit 'compression-invariance' principle and 'appendix-as-depth-layer' grammar are distinctive: they treat deck length as a selection/reuse problem rather than a redesign problem, and they document that strategy as a first-class design rule.
contribution: This file contributes a structured inventory of slide-level visual patterns, generator rules, component definitions, content budgets, and closing-grammar conventions that a fused design system can draw on to synthesize or audit consistent pitch-deck layouts.
summary_for_embedding: A structured design-substrate analysis of slides 35-49 of the ConceptV pitch-deck corpus, capturing per-slide attention flows, visual pattern recipes, reusable components, and generator rules for a warm gold-accented, slide-atomic pitch-deck grammar.
intention_for_embedding: To encode the closing and appendix visual language of the ConceptV pitch decks into reproducible design rules and reusable components for synthesis or review.
